articles.headline: MEA Rejects USCIRF Report, Cites Distorted Picture of India and Vandalism of Hindu Temples in U.S.
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of External Affairs (MEA) criticized the latest report by the U.S. Commission on International Religious Freedom (USCIRF).
• MEA spokesperson Randhir Jaiswal stated that USCIRF presents a "distorted and selective picture of India," relying on questionable sources and ideological narratives.
• The MEA urged USCIRF to address incidents of vandalism targeting Hindu temples in the United States instead of criticizing India.
• The USCIRF report had called for "targeted sanctions" against India's external intelligence agency, the Research & Analysis Wing (R&AW), and the Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh (RSS).
• These recommended sanctions included freezing assets and barring entry into the United States for individuals and entities deemed responsible for religious freedom violations.

India's strong rebuttal to the USCIRF report underscores its firm stance on national sovereignty and non-interference in internal affairs, a cornerstone of its foreign policy. The MEA's response highlights the challenges in diplomatic relations when external bodies issue reports perceived as biased or ideologically driven, potentially impacting India's international image and soft power. This incident also brings to the fore the complexities of religious freedom discourse in a globalized world, where domestic issues can become subjects of international scrutiny. India's call for reciprocity in addressing religious freedom concerns, by pointing to incidents in the U.S., reflects its commitment to equitable treatment and the principle of sovereign equality in international engagements, while also safeguarding its constitutional values.

articles.headline: National Quantum Mission Advances: 23 Institutions Approved for Quantum Labs
articles.explainer:
• Twenty-three academic institutions across India have received approval to establish quantum teaching laboratories.
• This initiative falls under the ambit of the National Quantum Mission (NQM), a flagship government program.
• The NQM was approved with a substantial budget of ₹6003.65 crore for the period 2023–2031.
• The mission's ambitious goals include developing quantum computers with capabilities ranging from 50 to 1,000 qubits.
• Other key objectives of the NQM are to achieve satellite-based secure quantum communication and develop high-precision quantum sensors and materials.
• An additional 100 proposals for quantum labs are currently under evaluation, indicating a broader push in this strategic technology.

The approval of quantum teaching laboratories under the National Quantum Mission signifies India's strategic commitment to fostering indigenous capabilities in cutting-edge technologies. This investment is crucial for national security, economic development, and maintaining technological sovereignty in the rapidly evolving global landscape. By focusing on quantum computing, secure communication, and advanced materials, India aims to position itself as a leader in the quantum domain, aligning with its vision of 'Atmanirbhar Bharat'. This initiative will not only drive research and innovation but also contribute to human resource development, creating a skilled workforce essential for leveraging the transformative potential of quantum science across various sectors, from healthcare to defense, thereby enhancing India's scientific temper and global competitiveness.

articles.headline: Geopolitical Risks Drive Oil Price Volatility, Impacting Global Energy Security
articles.explainer:
• Crude oil prices surged to $118 a barrel, reflecting heightened geopolitical risks, particularly from the West Asia conflict.
• This surge represents a more than 100% increase since mid-December 2025, when Brent crude was at $57.56.
• Unlike historical patterns where markets stabilized, current disruptions create tangible barriers to supply, fundamentally altering trade conditions.
• Key maritime chokepoints, including the Strait of Hormuz (20% of global oil consumption) and the Bab el-Mandeb/Suez corridor (10% of seaborne crude), are highly vulnerable.
• Geopolitical tensions inflate prices and increase maritime insecurity, leading to higher freight rates, insurance premiums, and tighter vessel availability.
• Oil has transformed into a political instrument, as seen with the redirection of Russian crude, introducing operational and financial volatility for importing nations.

The current volatility in global crude oil prices underscores the profound impact of geopolitical risks on energy security, moving beyond traditional supply-demand dynamics. For major importing economies like India, this shift has significant implications for fiscal stability, inflation management, and overall economic development. The vulnerability of critical maritime arteries highlights the imperative for diversified energy sources, robust supply chain resilience, and proactive diplomatic engagement to mitigate external shocks. This situation necessitates adaptable energy and trade strategies, emphasizing strategic petroleum reserves, renewable energy transition, and strengthening international partnerships. The transformation of oil into a political instrument further complicates global energy governance, demanding a nuanced foreign policy approach to safeguard national interests and ensure sustainable development.

articles.headline: Western Himalayas Experience Driest Spring in Four Centuries, Study Reveals
articles.explainer:
• Researchers from Birbal Sahni Institute of Palaeosciences (BSIP) and other institutions reconstructed 396 years of spring rainfall history in the Western Himalayas using tree cores from old deodar trees in Lahaul, Himachal Pradesh.
• The study, published recently, found that the spring of 2022 was the driest on record, and the last two decades represent the driest period in the entire 396-year reconstruction.
• Dendroclimatology, the science of determining past climates from tree rings, was employed, where tree ring width indicates rainfall abundance.
• The findings highlight significant implications for water security, disaster management, and agriculture across northern India, which heavily relies on Himalayan snowmelt.
• Permission from the Forest Department was obtained to extract core samples using an increment borer, ensuring minimal damage to the trees.

This scientific research underscores the escalating impact of climate change on sensitive ecosystems like the Western Himalayas, a critical water tower for the Indian subcontinent. The unprecedented drought conditions, as revealed by dendroclimatology, pose severe threats to regional water security, potentially exacerbating agrarian distress and increasing the frequency of climate-induced disasters. The findings necessitate a robust national climate action plan, integrating scientific data into policy formulation for sustainable development and disaster risk reduction. Addressing these environmental challenges requires inter-state cooperation and a focus on climate resilience, aligning with India's commitments to global environmental goals and ensuring the well-being of communities dependent on Himalayan resources.

articles.headline: Transgender Communities Protest Proposed Bill Redefining Gender Identity, Seek Withdrawal
articles.explainer:
• Transgender communities and civil society groups across India are protesting a proposed Union government legislation that seeks to withdraw the right to "self-perceived gender identity."
• The proposed Bill aims to redefine who a "transgender person" is, leading to widespread calls for its withdrawal and protection of self-perceived gender identity.
• Protests and public meetings have occurred in cities like New Delhi, Pune, Hyderabad, and Kolkata, with spontaneously formed coalitions planning campaigns.
• These campaigns include writing to Members of Parliament, organizing awareness and support group meetings, and strategizing opposition to the proposed legislation.
• An online public petition demanding the Bill's withdrawal has garnered over 13,000 signatures.

This widespread protest highlights critical issues concerning social justice, fundamental rights, and legislative processes in India. The right to self-perceived gender identity is a matter of human dignity and autonomy, affirmed by judicial precedents like the NALSA judgment. Any legislative attempt to redefine or restrict this right raises concerns about constitutionalism and the protection of vulnerable sections of society. The collective action by transgender communities and civil society underscores the importance of inclusive governance and participatory democracy, where marginalized voices are heard in policy-making. It also reflects the ongoing challenge of translating constitutional guarantees into effective legal frameworks that uphold the principles of equality and non-discrimination.

articles.trivia: The Supreme Court of India, in its landmark 2014 NALSA judgment, recognized transgender persons as the 'third gender' and affirmed their right to self-identification of gender.

articles.headline: Israel Envoy Affirms No 'Perpetual War' with Iran; India's Diplomacy Secures Ship Passage
articles.explainer:
• Israel's Ambassador to India, Reuven Azar, stated that Israel does not seek "perpetual war" with Iran and remains open to diplomatic solutions if Iran "changes course."
• External Affairs Minister S. Jaishankar confirmed that India's talks with Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi have "yielded some results" in securing the safe passage of two LNG-laden ships through the Strait of Hormuz.
• The conflict in West Asia entered its third week, following joint U.S. and Israel strikes that reportedly killed Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khameini on February 28.
• India's EAM emphasized the importance of reasoning and coordination for solutions, suggesting a global benefit from such an approach.
• The Iranian government has reportedly rejected current talks or a ceasefire, accusing the U.S. of undermining dialogue.

This development highlights the complex geopolitical dynamics in West Asia and India's proactive role in navigating regional conflicts to safeguard its national interests. India's diplomatic engagement with Iran to ensure the safe passage of merchant vessels through the Strait of Hormuz is crucial for maintaining energy security and protecting global supply chains. The EAM's emphasis on dialogue underscores India's commitment to multilateralism and peaceful resolution of disputes, positioning it as a responsible global actor. The ongoing conflict, however, continues to pose significant challenges to regional stability, impacting international trade and necessitating sustained diplomatic efforts to de-escalate tensions and foster a conducive environment for economic cooperation.

articles.headline: India's Neighbourhood Diplomacy Tested by Escalating West Asia Conflict
articles.explainer:
• The Iranian warship IRIS Dena was reportedly sunk by the United States in the Indian Ocean on March 4, marking an escalation of the West Asia conflict.
• This conflict, which commenced on February 28, has impacted South Asia by disrupting trade, availability of essential goods like food and fuel, and the safety of citizens.
• Approximately 25 million South Asians, including 10 million Indians, reside and work in the West Asia region, making their safety a significant concern.
• Indians constitute about 15% of the global seafarer population, placing them at heightened risk, particularly in strategic maritime choke points like the Strait of Hormuz.
• India's initial response, sending Foreign Secretary Vikram Misri to the Iranian Embassy after five days, contrasted with the immediate condolences issued by Bangladesh, Pakistan, Maldives, and Sri Lanka following the U.S.-Israel strikes that killed Iran’s Supreme Leader.
• The conflict involved U.S.-Israel strikes against Iran and subsequent counter-attacks by Iran, leading to regional instability.

This escalating conflict in West Asia presents a complex challenge to India's neighbourhood diplomacy and its strategic autonomy. The significant Indian diaspora in the region necessitates a proactive foreign policy focused on their welfare and safe evacuation if required. Furthermore, the disruption to maritime trade routes, especially through the Strait of Hormuz, has direct implications for India's energy security and economic stability. India's nuanced diplomatic stance, balancing its historical ties with West Asian nations and its strategic partnerships with global powers, is crucial for maintaining regional stability and protecting its national interests. The situation underscores the interconnectedness of global geopolitics with India's domestic economic and security concerns, demanding a calibrated approach to foreign policy.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Calcutta HC Order for NIA Probe into Beldanga Violence, Questions UAPA Invocation
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court on March 16, 2026, declined to intervene in a Calcutta High Court decision that confirmed a National Investigation Agency (NIA) probe into violence in Beldanga, Murshidabad, West Bengal.
• The Calcutta High Court, on February 27, had directed the West Bengal government to hand over case records to the NIA and mandated the agency to file a status report by March 24.
• West Bengal challenged the High Court's directive, referencing the Supreme Court's earlier reservations (February 11) regarding the invocation of the Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act (UAPA) in the case.
• On February 11, the apex court had questioned whether an "emotional outburst" could be classified as a "terrorist act" under UAPA without sufficient material evidence.
• The violence erupted on January 16 in Beldanga following the death of a migrant worker, leading to widespread public unrest, blockades of railway tracks and a national highway, and injuries.
• The Union Ministry of Home Affairs had previously directed the NIA to take over the investigation, citing various statutes including the UAPA, West Bengal Maintenance of Public Order Act, and Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita.

This Supreme Court decision clarifies the delicate balance between judicial oversight and the operational autonomy of central investigative agencies, particularly concerning the invocation of stringent laws like the UAPA. It underscores the judiciary's role in ensuring fair investigation while also scrutinizing the application of anti-terror legislation to prevent potential overreach. The interplay between the High Court's confirmation of the NIA probe and the Supreme Court's earlier reservations on UAPA highlights the complexities of federalism in security matters and the constitutional principles governing the separation of powers. This case is significant for understanding the evolving jurisprudence on internal security, civil liberties, and the Centre-State dynamics in law enforcement.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Reaffirms Right to Die with Dignity, Approves Withdrawal of Life Support
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court of India, in a landmark decision, approved the withdrawal of life support, including artificial nutrition and hydration, for Harish Rana.
• Harish Rana had been in a persistent vegetative state (PVS) for over 12 years following a head injury.
• The Court invoked the constitutional idea of dignity, stating that keeping terminally ill patients in PVS alive via technology is incompatible with dignity.
• This judgment expands the right to live with dignity under Article 21 of the Constitution to encompass the right to die with dignity.
• The Court also endorsed the concept of a 'living will', a legal document allowing individuals to state preferences for medical treatment if incapacitated.
• Previous landmark cases cited include Smt. Gian Kaur vs The State Of Punjab (1996) and the Aruna Shanbaug petition (2011), with a 2018 Constitution Bench ruling affirming the right to die with dignity.

The Supreme Court's latest pronouncement on the right to die with dignity is a significant step in upholding individual autonomy and human dignity, deeply rooted in Article 21 of the Constitution. By allowing the withdrawal of life support for patients in a persistent vegetative state, the judiciary has provided a compassionate framework for end-of-life care, addressing the ethical dilemmas posed by advanced medical technology. This judgment reinforces the principle of constitutionalism, ensuring that fundamental rights evolve to meet contemporary challenges. The endorsement of 'living wills' empowers citizens to make informed choices about their medical treatment, promoting patient-centric healthcare and reducing the burden on families. It also highlights the judiciary's role in filling legislative gaps in sensitive areas, balancing the sanctity of life with the right to a dignified death, and fostering a more humane approach to terminal illness.

articles.headline: Global Economic and Geopolitical Shifts: India's FDI, China's Plan, US Diplomacy
articles.explainer:
• Reports indicate a possible delay in former US President Donald Trump's visit to China, impacting geopolitical dynamics.
• India has introduced changes to its Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) policy, signaling potential shifts in its economic strategy.
• China is reportedly preparing to unveil its new Five-Year Plan, outlining its economic and social development goals for the coming period.
• These developments collectively highlight significant movements in international relations and economic policy across major global powers.
• The confluence of these events suggests evolving trade relations, investment landscapes, and strategic alliances.

The simultaneous developments concerning a potential delay in high-level US-China diplomatic engagement, India's adjustments to its Foreign Direct Investment policy, and China's upcoming Five-Year Plan underscore the dynamic nature of global geopolitics and economic governance. India's FDI policy changes are crucial for attracting capital, fostering economic growth, and integrating into global supply chains, reflecting its commitment to economic reforms and ease of doing business. Concurrently, China's Five-Year Plan will dictate its trajectory in areas like technological innovation, environmental sustainability, and social welfare, influencing global trade and investment patterns. The US-China diplomatic shifts, meanwhile, have broader implications for international stability, trade wars, and the balance of power, affecting multilateral institutions and global economic recovery efforts. These interconnected events necessitate a comprehensive understanding of international political economy and strategic foresight for policymakers.

articles.headline: NavIC Satellite Experiences Atomic Clock Failure, Impact on India's Regional Navigation System
articles.explainer:
• One of the satellites in India's NavIC constellation has reportedly experienced a failure in its onboard atomic clock.
• Atomic clocks are crucial for precise timing, which is fundamental for the accurate functioning of satellite navigation systems.
• NavIC (Navigation with Indian Constellation) is India's independent regional navigation satellite system, developed by ISRO.
• It provides accurate real-time positioning and timing services over India and a region extending up to 1,500 km around the Indian mainland.
• The system currently comprises 7 operational satellites, with 3 in geostationary orbit and 4 in geosynchronous orbit.
• A faulty atomic clock can degrade the accuracy of the satellite's signals, potentially affecting the overall performance and reliability of the constellation.

The reported failure of an atomic clock on a NavIC satellite underscores the inherent technological complexities and vulnerabilities in developing indigenous space infrastructure. NavIC, a cornerstone of India's strategic autonomy in critical technology, aims to reduce reliance on foreign global navigation satellite systems like GPS. This incident highlights the imperative for robust redundancy mechanisms and advanced materials science in space-grade components. While such setbacks are common in advanced technological pursuits, they necessitate a re-evaluation of quality control and mission resilience strategies. India's commitment to self-reliance in space technology, crucial for both civilian applications and national security, demands continuous innovation and investment in research and development to overcome such challenges and ensure the long-term operational integrity of its navigation system.

articles.headline: NATO Allies Reject US Demand for Hormuz Armada, Highlighting Transatlantic Divisions
articles.explainer:
• Major NATO allies, including Germany and France, reportedly rejected a demand from the then-US administration (under Donald Trump) to send a naval armada to the Strait of Hormuz.
• The US had sought increased European military presence in the Persian Gulf to counter perceived threats from Iran.
• European nations expressed concerns that such a deployment could escalate tensions in the region and preferred a diplomatic approach.
• The Strait of Hormuz is a critical chokepoint for global oil shipments, with approximately one-fifth of the world's oil passing through it.
• The rejection highlighted growing transatlantic divisions and differing foreign policy priorities between the US and its European allies regarding Iran.

The refusal by key NATO allies to support a US-led naval deployment in the Strait of Hormuz underscores significant divergences in transatlantic foreign policy and security doctrines. This incident reflects the complexities of multilateral alliances, where national interests and strategic assessments can lead to differing approaches to global flashpoints. For India, a major energy importer, stability in the Persian Gulf and the unimpeded flow of oil through the Strait of Hormuz are paramount for its energy security. The episode highlights the delicate balance between coercive diplomacy and de-escalation in international relations, and the potential for unilateral actions by major powers to strain alliances and complicate regional stability, impacting global trade and economic interconnectedness.

articles.headline: Global Implications of US Policy on Strait of Hormuz and Iran Tensions
articles.explainer:
• The US administration's (under Donald Trump) assertive stance towards Iran, particularly concerning its nuclear program and regional activities, significantly heightened tensions in the Persian Gulf.
• The Strait of Hormuz, a narrow waterway connecting the Persian Gulf to the Arabian Sea, became a focal point for these tensions due to its strategic importance for global oil trade.
• Disruptions or threats to shipping in the Strait of Hormuz have immediate and far-reaching consequences for international energy markets and global economic stability.
• The US withdrawal from the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action (JCPOA) and subsequent imposition of sanctions on Iran were key drivers of the escalating crisis.
• The situation prompted concerns among international allies and trading partners about the potential for military confrontation and its ripple effects on global supply chains.

The escalating tensions surrounding the Strait of Hormuz, driven by the US's foreign policy approach towards Iran, exemplify how regional conflicts can rapidly acquire global dimensions. This geopolitical flashpoint, critical for international trade and energy security, highlights the interconnectedness of the global economy and the profound impact of major power politics on developing nations like India. The challenges to the rules-based international order, coupled with the complexities of non-proliferation and regional power dynamics, necessitate a nuanced diplomatic engagement from all stakeholders. For India, ensuring the stability of maritime trade routes and maintaining cordial relations with all parties in the Persian Gulf region is vital for its economic growth and strategic interests, underscoring the need for multilateral solutions to de-escalate such crises.

articles.headline: CITU Criticizes Centre's Disinvestment Policies for Visakhapatnam Steel Plant
articles.explainer:
• The Centre of Indian Trade Unions (CITU) has criticized the Central government's policies regarding the Visakhapatnam Steel Plant (VSP).
• CITU alleges that the Centre is prioritizing employee removal over production increase and is deliberately weakening VSP for privatization.
• They claim that Centre's policies have led to VSP incurring losses of ₹5,000 crore last year and cessation of exports since 2021.
• CITU states that wage revisions, salaries, HRA, bonuses, incentives, and loans for workers have been withheld for the past five years.
• Workers are reportedly forced to work on half salaries despite efforts to save the plant, demanding implementation of labour rights.

The allegations by CITU regarding the Visakhapatnam Steel Plant's privatization highlight critical aspects of India's economic policy, particularly disinvestment and its social implications. The debate over public sector undertakings (PSUs) often revolves around efficiency, strategic assets, and employment generation versus fiscal prudence and market liberalization. This situation underscores the tension between economic reforms aimed at reducing the fiscal deficit and the commitment to social welfare and labour rights. It brings to the fore questions of industrial policy, the role of trade unions in safeguarding worker interests, and the broader impact of privatization on regional economies and national industrial capacity. Ensuring a balanced approach that considers both economic viability and social equity is crucial for sustainable development and inclusive growth.
gsSubTopics: Social Justice: Labour Laws and Rights

articles.headline: EAM Jaishankar Engages EU Counterparts on West Asia, Ukraine, and Trade Deal
articles.explainer:
• External Affairs Minister S. Jaishankar held discussions with European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen and EU Foreign Ministers in Brussels.
• Key topics included the geopolitical situations in West Asia and Ukraine, emphasizing shared objectives of de-escalation, stability, and energy security.
• Discussions also focused on the swift implementation of the India-EU trade deal, signed in January, covering trade, investment, technology, mobility, and defence.
• The visit underscored India's active diplomatic engagement with the 27-member European bloc on critical global and bilateral issues.
• Related discussions touched upon the Strait of Hormuz, highlighting its significance for energy security and global supply chains.

India's proactive diplomatic outreach to the European Union reflects its commitment to multi-alignment and strengthening strategic partnerships amidst evolving global power dynamics. The engagement on West Asia and Ukraine underscores India's role in promoting regional stability and its vested interest in global energy security, given its significant reliance on crude oil imports. The emphasis on accelerating the trade deal highlights India's economic diplomacy, aiming to enhance bilateral trade and investment, crucial for economic development and technological advancement. Such high-level interactions are vital for shaping India's foreign policy trajectory and reinforcing its position as a responsible global actor, contributing to a more multipolar world order and fostering cooperative federalism in international relations.

Significance of India-EU strategic partnership for trade, technology, and security.

articles.headline: Indian Navy Escorts Merchant Vessels Through Gulf of Oman Under Operation Sankalp
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Navy has deployed warships to escort Indian-flagged merchant vessels transiting through the Persian Gulf region.
• Three Indian Navy ships escorted merchant vessels Shivalik, Nanda Devi, and Jag Laadki from the Gulf of Oman after they crossed the Strait of Hormuz.
• This deployment ensures the safe transit of Indian vessels through vulnerable international waters.
• The warships are operating under "Operation Sankalp," a mission specifically aimed at safeguarding merchant shipping and protecting India’s maritime interests in the Gulf region.
• The move comes amidst heightened geopolitical tensions and security concerns in the West Asian maritime domain.

This proactive measure by the Indian Navy under Operation Sankalp underscores India's commitment to securing its maritime trade routes and protecting its economic interests in volatile international waters. The Persian Gulf and Gulf of Oman are critical for India's energy imports and trade, making freedom of navigation paramount. Such deployments are vital for maintaining regional stability and projecting India's naval capabilities as a net security provider in the Indian Ocean Region. This initiative also reflects India's strategic autonomy in safeguarding its assets amidst geopolitical uncertainties, reinforcing the importance of a robust maritime security architecture for national development and global trade.
gsSubTopics: Security: Challenges to Internal Security through Communication Networks
International Relations: India's Interests in West Asia

mainsPoints: Significance of maritime security for India's economic and strategic interests.
Challenges to freedom of navigation in volatile regions.

articles.trivia: Operation Sankalp was launched by the Indian Navy in June 2019 in the Persian Gulf and Gulf of Oman to reassure Indian-flagged vessels navigating through the region following attacks on merchant ships.

articles.headline: West Asian Crisis Impacts Kerala's Wellness Tourism, Raises Energy Security Concerns
articles.explainer:
• The ongoing West Asian conflict has severely impacted Kerala's hospitality sector, particularly wellness tourism, which contributes approximately 10% to the state's GDP.
• Kerala is a major hub for wellness tourism, with nearly 70% of its foreign tourists belonging to this category.
• Around 85% of foreign visitors to Kerala use Doha or Dubai as transit points, and travel restrictions due to the conflict have led to significant cancellations and postponements.
• The sector has already lost 20-25% of its business for the current season (October-March) and faces a lack of bookings for the next season.
• An associated challenge is the shortage of commercial LPG, forcing the closure of over 100 hotels in Kochi, indicating broader energy security vulnerabilities.

This situation underscores the economic vulnerability of Indian states to global geopolitical events, particularly those impacting crucial sectors like tourism and energy. Kerala's significant reliance on international wellness tourism, coupled with its dependence on West Asian transit hubs, exposes the need for greater economic diversification and resilience strategies. The resulting commercial LPG shortage highlights broader energy security challenges for India, emphasizing the importance of robust supply chain management and alternative energy sources. This incident serves as a critical case study for understanding the cascading effects of international conflicts on domestic economic development and the imperative for comprehensive policy responses to mitigate such external shocks.
gsSubTopics: Economy: Indian Economy & Issues relating to Planning, Mobilization of Resources, Growth, Development and Employment
International Relations: Effect of Policies & Politics of Developed & Developing Countries on India's Interests

articles.trivia: Ayurveda, a traditional system of medicine with historical roots in India, is a significant component of Kerala's wellness tourism offerings, attracting international visitors seeking holistic health treatments.

articles.headline: Trinamool MPs Protest Election Commission's Removal of West Bengal Officials in Rajya Sabha
articles.explainer:
• Trinamool Congress (TMC) MPs walked out of the Rajya Sabha on March 16, 2026, boycotting the day's sitting, to protest the Election Commission's (EC) removal of top bureaucrats in West Bengal.
• The EC had directed the removal of the Chief Secretary, Principal Secretary, and Home Secretary in West Bengal ahead of the Assembly polls in the state.
• TMC Rajya Sabha leader Derek O'Brien stated that while the EC has the power to do so, the party was protesting the manner and timing ("dead of night") of the removals.
• Parliamentary Affairs Minister Kiren Rijiju condemned the walkout, emphasizing that questioning a constitutional authority like the Election Commission in such a manner is not advisable.
• The incident highlights the powers of the Election Commission in ensuring free and fair elections and the dynamics between constitutional bodies and political parties.

This event underscores the critical role and constitutional autonomy of the Election Commission of India in upholding the integrity of the electoral process. The EC's power to transfer administrative officials during elections is a significant aspect of its mandate to ensure free and fair polls, often impacting Centre-State administrative relations. While parliamentary protests are a legitimate tool in a vibrant democracy, the debate surrounding the questioning of a constitutional authority's decisions raises important questions about parliamentary decorum and the separation of powers. This incident highlights the delicate balance between the executive, legislative, and independent constitutional bodies in India's federal structure, especially during election cycles, and its implications for good governance and electoral reforms.

Role of EC in ensuring free and fair elections.
Parliamentary protests against constitutional body decisions.
mainsPoints: Analyzing the extent of Election Commission's powers and its impact on state administration.
Debate on parliamentary decorum and questioning constitutional authorities.
Implications for federal structure and Centre-State administrative relations during elections.

articles.trivia: Article 324 of the Indian Constitution vests the superintendence, direction, and control of the preparation of electoral rolls and the conduct of all elections to Parliament and to the Legislature of every State in the Election Commission.

articles.headline: Analysis of Subdued Gold Prices in India Amidst West Asian Conflict
articles.explainer:
• Gold prices in India have remained subdued despite the ongoing geopolitical instability stemming from the West Asian conflict.
• Traditionally, gold is considered a safe-haven asset, with its value typically appreciating during periods of global uncertainty and geopolitical tensions.
• The article aims to explore the specific domestic and international factors contributing to this unusual trend in the Indian gold market.
• Potential contributing factors could include a relatively strong Indian Rupee against major currencies, which makes gold imports cheaper in local terms.
• Other influences might involve specific government policies, such as import duties on gold, or shifts in domestic demand and supply dynamics.
• The analysis likely considers how global gold price movements might be decoupling from or being offset by unique conditions within the Indian market.

This phenomenon of subdued gold prices in India amidst global geopolitical turmoil highlights the complex interplay of international and domestic economic forces. While global events often drive a flight to safety, India's internal market dynamics, including currency strength, import regulations, and local demand patterns, can significantly influence asset prices. Understanding this divergence is crucial for comprehending macroeconomic stability, investor behavior, and the effectiveness of fiscal policy measures related to commodities. It also sheds light on the role of gold as a traditional store of value in Indian households and how its perceived hedging capabilities might be affected by contemporary economic realities, influencing investment models and resource mobilization.

articles.trivia: India is one of the largest consumers of gold globally, with a significant portion of its demand driven by cultural and traditional practices, especially during festivals and weddings.

articles.headline: Madras High Court Dismisses Plea on Temple Land Arrears, Upholds HR&CE Act Provision
articles.explainer:
• The Madras High Court dismissed a writ appeal filed by Amrutanjan Limited concerning rental arrears for land belonging to the Mylapore Kapaleeswarar Temple.
• The Hindu Religious and Charitable Endowments (HR&CE) department informed the court that Amrutanjan owed ₹9.74 crore in arrears for occupying 14 grounds of temple land until 2018.
• The land was originally leased for a 99-year period in 1901 at a monthly rent of ₹1,400, with Amrutanjan acquiring the lease rights from an assignee.
• Amrutanjan continued to occupy the property even after the 99-year lease expired on August 27, 2000, and subsequently challenged Section 34A(5) of the Tamil Nadu HR&CE Act, 1959.
• Section 34A(5) mandates the deposit of the claimed rental amount as a precondition for filing a statutory appeal before the HR&CE Commissioner.
• The High Court found no evidence that Amrutanjan had actually filed the statutory appeal or that the Commissioner had insisted on the deposit, leading to the dismissal of the writ appeal.

This Madras High Court judgment reinforces the statutory powers of state HR&CE departments in managing religious endowments and recovering outstanding dues, highlighting the critical importance of procedural adherence in legal challenges. It underscores the state's constitutional responsibility to protect public and institutional assets, particularly those belonging to religious trusts, against prolonged unauthorized occupation. The case also delves into principles of property law, the enforceability of lease agreements, and the legal framework governing religious institutions, ensuring accountability and financial integrity in their administration. The dismissal on grounds of non-compliance with statutory appeal requirements emphasizes the judiciary's role in upholding legal processes and preventing frivolous litigation.

Polity: Statutory, Regulatory and various Quasi-judicial Bodies
Polity: Property Rights and Constitutional Provisions

articles.headline: Regional Group Protests Railway Exam Language Policy, Demands Kannada Inclusion
articles.explainer:
• The Karnataka Rakshana Vedike (KRV) announced protests to disrupt Railway promotion exams in Bengaluru and Hubballi on Tuesday.
• The protest is against the South Western Railway conducting exams only in Hindi and English, excluding Kannada.
• The exams are for 295 posts, including Goods Train Manager (60% promotional quota) and Limited Departmental Competitive Examination (LDCE) (15% promotional quota).
• Previously, following protests in 2008, the Railway Examination Board had begun holding exams in local languages, including Kannada, a practice now reportedly reversed.
• KRV president T. A. Narayana Gowda stated the protest would proceed despite a police notice, demanding resignation of Union Ministers.

This incident highlights the persistent tension between national language policies and regional linguistic aspirations, a critical aspect of India's federal structure and linguistic diversity. The demand for conducting central government recruitment examinations in regional languages underscores the principle of inclusive governance and equal opportunity, particularly for candidates from non-Hindi speaking states. The reversal of a previous policy allowing local languages in Railway exams raises questions about the Centre-State relations and the implementation of the Official Languages Act. Such issues often spark debates on cultural identity, administrative efficiency, and the constitutional guarantees related to language rights, impacting social cohesion and regional development. Ensuring equitable access to central government jobs across all linguistic groups is vital for fostering national integration and preventing regional alienation.

Railway Recruitment Boards (RRBs) conduct exams for various posts.

articles.headline: Centre's Policy on Use of Beacon Lights on Official Vehicles Re-examined
articles.explainer:
• In April 2017, the Union Cabinet banned the use of red beacon lights (lal batti) on all official vehicles, including those of the Prime Minister, President, and Chief Ministers.
• The ban came into effect from May 1, 2017, aiming to end VIP culture and promote equality.
• Only emergency services like ambulances, fire tenders, and police vehicles are permitted to use multi-coloured flashing lights.
• The Motor Vehicles Act, 1988, and subsequent rules empower the Central Government to specify who can use such lights.
• The decision was taken under the powers conferred by Section 109 of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988.

The prohibition on red beacon lights by the Union government in 2017 marked a significant step towards dismantling the vestiges of colonial-era VIP culture and fostering a sense of equality among citizens. This policy decision, rooted in principles of good governance and administrative reform, aimed to enhance public trust in institutions by demonstrating that no individual is above the law. While the move was widely lauded for promoting democratic values, its effective implementation across all levels of government, including state and local bodies, remains a continuous challenge. The debate often resurfaces when local officials are perceived to flout these national guidelines, highlighting the complexities of federal governance and the need for consistent enforcement to uphold the spirit of the law and ensure accountability in public life.

Exceptions: emergency services (ambulances, police, fire)

articles.trivia: The use of red beacon lights was initially introduced during the British Raj to distinguish high-ranking officials.

articles.headline: Understanding 'No Quarter': Implications in International Humanitarian Law
articles.explainer:
• "No Quarter" is a historical military term signifying a refusal to spare the lives of combatants who surrender, are captured, or are otherwise rendered hors de combat.
• Under modern international humanitarian law (IHL), specifically the Geneva Conventions and their Additional Protocols, giving "no quarter" is strictly prohibited.
• Combatants who surrender or are captured are considered prisoners of war (POWs) and are entitled to protection from violence, ill-treatment, and murder.
• The principle of distinction, proportionality, and humanity are fundamental tenets of IHL, aiming to limit the effects of armed conflict.
• Commanders who order or allow "no quarter" can be prosecuted for war crimes under international criminal law.

The concept of "no quarter," while historically present in warfare, stands in stark contrast to the foundational principles of modern international humanitarian law. Its prohibition is a critical aspect of the Geneva Conventions, which seek to humanize armed conflict by protecting non-combatants and those who are no longer participating in hostilities. This legal framework reflects a global consensus on the imperative to uphold human dignity even amidst the brutality of war, emphasizing the principles of distinction, proportionality, and humanity. Violations of this prohibition constitute war crimes, underscoring the accountability mechanisms within international criminal justice. Understanding such concepts is vital for appreciating the evolution of international law and its ongoing efforts to mitigate the suffering caused by armed conflicts and ensure respect for human rights.

International Relations: India and its neighborhood- relations

mainsPoints: Evolution of laws of armed conflict

articles.trivia: The phrase "no quarter" originated from the practice of giving a captured enemy soldier "quarter" or lodging, which was denied if no quarter was given.

articles.headline: Anna University and Ramco Systems Collaborate for Advanced ERP Laboratory
articles.explainer:
• Anna University and Ramco Systems have signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) to establish an Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) laboratory.
• The ERP lab will be set up at Anna University, specifically for its Industrial Engineering programme.
• Under the MoU, Ramco Systems will provide students with access to its cloud-based ERP platform using secured login credentials.
• The agreement outlines a structured framework encompassing faculty enablement, industry-oriented training, and skill development initiatives.
• It also includes provisions for research and academic events, fostering a robust industry-academia linkage.
• The MoU was signed by V. Kumaresan, Registrar-in-charge, Anna University, and Ravi Kula Chandran R., CFO, Ramco Systems.

This collaboration between Anna University and Ramco Systems signifies a crucial step towards bridging the skill gap between academia and industry, a key objective of national education policy and skill development missions. By integrating a cloud-based ERP platform into the Industrial Engineering curriculum, students will gain practical exposure to cutting-edge enterprise software, enhancing their employability and readiness for the digital economy. This initiative aligns with the broader vision of fostering innovation and technological literacy among youth, contributing to human capital development. Such partnerships exemplify cooperative federalism in action, where state-level institutions contribute to national development goals. Furthermore, it underscores the importance of public-private partnerships in advancing educational infrastructure and promoting industry-relevant research, crucial for India's economic growth and global competitiveness in the Fourth Industrial Revolution.
gsSubTopics: Science & Technology: Developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Economy: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment

articles.trivia: The first ERP system was developed in the 1960s, initially known as Material Requirements Planning (MRP), which later evolved into Manufacturing Resource Planning (MRP II) before becoming comprehensive Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) systems in the 1990s.

articles.headline: Tamil Writer Jayamohan Institutes 'Living Tamil Award for World Literature'
articles.explainer:
• Tamil writer Jayamohan has announced the establishment of the 'Living Tamil Award for World Literature'.
• The award carries a prize amount of ₹1 crore, equivalent to approximately $100,000, making it India's largest literary award.
• It will recognize outstanding works in both world and Indian literature.
• The winner will be announced simultaneously in New Delhi and New York City.
• Jayamohan stated the initiative was partly in response to the conferment of the Jnanpith Award on lyricist Vairamuthu.

The institution of a significant literary award with a substantial prize money underscores the growing private patronage for arts and culture in India. This initiative, aiming to recognize both Indian and world literature, reflects a broader cultural aspiration to elevate Indian literary contributions on a global stage, fostering cross-cultural dialogue and intellectual exchange. Such private sector involvement complements government efforts in promoting cultural heritage and artistic expression, aligning with the objectives of cultural diplomacy and soft power projection. It also highlights the dynamic landscape of literary recognition, where new platforms emerge to celebrate diverse voices and narratives, contributing to the richness of the nation's cultural fabric and intellectual discourse.
gsSubTopics: Indian Culture: Salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times
Social: Role of Civil Society in Development
prelimsPoints: Living Tamil Award for World Literature: ₹1 crore prize
Aims to be India's largest literary award
Recognizes Indian and world literature
mainsPoints: Role of private initiatives in cultural promotion
Impact of literary awards on cultural landscape
India's soft power through literature
articles.imageDescription: A stack of diverse books with a traditional Indian lamp, symbolizing literature and culture.
articles.trivia: The Jnanpith Award, India's highest literary honour, was instituted in 1961 and is given for outstanding contribution to literature by an Indian writer in any of the 22 languages recognized by the Eighth Schedule of the Indian Constitution.

GS2.headline: MEA Rejects USCIRF Report, Cites Distorted Picture of India and Vandalism of Hindu Temples in U.S.
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of External Affairs (MEA) criticized the latest report by the U.S. Commission on International Religious Freedom (USCIRF).
• MEA spokesperson Randhir Jaiswal stated that USCIRF presents a "distorted and selective picture of India," relying on questionable sources and ideological narratives.
• The MEA urged USCIRF to address incidents of vandalism targeting Hindu temples in the United States instead of criticizing India.
• The USCIRF report had called for "targeted sanctions" against India's external intelligence agency, the Research & Analysis Wing (R&AW), and the Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh (RSS).
• These recommended sanctions included freezing assets and barring entry into the United States for individuals and entities deemed responsible for religious freedom violations.

India's strong rebuttal to the USCIRF report underscores its firm stance on national sovereignty and non-interference in internal affairs, a cornerstone of its foreign policy. The MEA's response highlights the challenges in diplomatic relations when external bodies issue reports perceived as biased or ideologically driven, potentially impacting India's international image and soft power. This incident also brings to the fore the complexities of religious freedom discourse in a globalized world, where domestic issues can become subjects of international scrutiny. India's call for reciprocity in addressing religious freedom concerns, by pointing to incidents in the U.S., reflects its commitment to equitable treatment and the principle of sovereign equality in international engagements, while also safeguarding its constitutional values.

Governance: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors

GS2.headline: Transgender Communities Protest Proposed Bill Redefining Gender Identity, Seek Withdrawal
GS2.explainer:
• Transgender communities and civil society groups across India are protesting a proposed Union government legislation that seeks to withdraw the right to "self-perceived gender identity."
• The proposed Bill aims to redefine who a "transgender person" is, leading to widespread calls for its withdrawal and protection of self-perceived gender identity.
• Protests and public meetings have occurred in cities like New Delhi, Pune, Hyderabad, and Kolkata, with spontaneously formed coalitions planning campaigns.
• These campaigns include writing to Members of Parliament, organizing awareness and support group meetings, and strategizing opposition to the proposed legislation.
• An online public petition demanding the Bill's withdrawal has garnered over 13,000 signatures.

This widespread protest highlights critical issues concerning social justice, fundamental rights, and legislative processes in India. The right to self-perceived gender identity is a matter of human dignity and autonomy, affirmed by judicial precedents like the NALSA judgment. Any legislative attempt to redefine or restrict this right raises concerns about constitutionalism and the protection of vulnerable sections of society. The collective action by transgender communities and civil society underscores the importance of inclusive governance and participatory democracy, where marginalized voices are heard in policy-making. It also reflects the ongoing challenge of translating constitutional guarantees into effective legal frameworks that uphold the principles of equality and non-discrimination.

GS2.headline: Israel Envoy Affirms No 'Perpetual War' with Iran; India's Diplomacy Secures Ship Passage
GS2.explainer:
• Israel's Ambassador to India, Reuven Azar, stated that Israel does not seek "perpetual war" with Iran and remains open to diplomatic solutions if Iran "changes course."
• External Affairs Minister S. Jaishankar confirmed that India's talks with Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi have "yielded some results" in securing the safe passage of two LNG-laden ships through the Strait of Hormuz.
• The conflict in West Asia entered its third week, following joint U.S. and Israel strikes that reportedly killed Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khameini on February 28.
• India's EAM emphasized the importance of reasoning and coordination for solutions, suggesting a global benefit from such an approach.
• The Iranian government has reportedly rejected current talks or a ceasefire, accusing the U.S. of undermining dialogue.

This development highlights the complex geopolitical dynamics in West Asia and India's proactive role in navigating regional conflicts to safeguard its national interests. India's diplomatic engagement with Iran to ensure the safe passage of merchant vessels through the Strait of Hormuz is crucial for maintaining energy security and protecting global supply chains. The EAM's emphasis on dialogue underscores India's commitment to multilateralism and peaceful resolution of disputes, positioning it as a responsible global actor. The ongoing conflict, however, continues to pose significant challenges to regional stability, impacting international trade and necessitating sustained diplomatic efforts to de-escalate tensions and foster a conducive environment for economic cooperation.

GS2.headline: India's Neighbourhood Diplomacy Tested by Escalating West Asia Conflict
GS2.explainer:
• The Iranian warship IRIS Dena was reportedly sunk by the United States in the Indian Ocean on March 4, marking an escalation of the West Asia conflict.
• This conflict, which commenced on February 28, has impacted South Asia by disrupting trade, availability of essential goods like food and fuel, and the safety of citizens.
• Approximately 25 million South Asians, including 10 million Indians, reside and work in the West Asia region, making their safety a significant concern.
• Indians constitute about 15% of the global seafarer population, placing them at heightened risk, particularly in strategic maritime choke points like the Strait of Hormuz.
• India's initial response, sending Foreign Secretary Vikram Misri to the Iranian Embassy after five days, contrasted with the immediate condolences issued by Bangladesh, Pakistan, Maldives, and Sri Lanka following the U.S.-Israel strikes that killed Iran’s Supreme Leader.
• The conflict involved U.S.-Israel strikes against Iran and subsequent counter-attacks by Iran, leading to regional instability.

This escalating conflict in West Asia presents a complex challenge to India's neighbourhood diplomacy and its strategic autonomy. The significant Indian diaspora in the region necessitates a proactive foreign policy focused on their welfare and safe evacuation if required. Furthermore, the disruption to maritime trade routes, especially through the Strait of Hormuz, has direct implications for India's energy security and economic stability. India's nuanced diplomatic stance, balancing its historical ties with West Asian nations and its strategic partnerships with global powers, is crucial for maintaining regional stability and protecting its national interests. The situation underscores the interconnectedness of global geopolitics with India's domestic economic and security concerns, demanding a calibrated approach to foreign policy.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Calcutta HC Order for NIA Probe into Beldanga Violence, Questions UAPA Invocation
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court on March 16, 2026, declined to intervene in a Calcutta High Court decision that confirmed a National Investigation Agency (NIA) probe into violence in Beldanga, Murshidabad, West Bengal.
• The Calcutta High Court, on February 27, had directed the West Bengal government to hand over case records to the NIA and mandated the agency to file a status report by March 24.
• West Bengal challenged the High Court's directive, referencing the Supreme Court's earlier reservations (February 11) regarding the invocation of the Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act (UAPA) in the case.
• On February 11, the apex court had questioned whether an "emotional outburst" could be classified as a "terrorist act" under UAPA without sufficient material evidence.
• The violence erupted on January 16 in Beldanga following the death of a migrant worker, leading to widespread public unrest, blockades of railway tracks and a national highway, and injuries.
• The Union Ministry of Home Affairs had previously directed the NIA to take over the investigation, citing various statutes including the UAPA, West Bengal Maintenance of Public Order Act, and Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita.

This Supreme Court decision clarifies the delicate balance between judicial oversight and the operational autonomy of central investigative agencies, particularly concerning the invocation of stringent laws like the UAPA. It underscores the judiciary's role in ensuring fair investigation while also scrutinizing the application of anti-terror legislation to prevent potential overreach. The interplay between the High Court's confirmation of the NIA probe and the Supreme Court's earlier reservations on UAPA highlights the complexities of federalism in security matters and the constitutional principles governing the separation of powers. This case is significant for understanding the evolving jurisprudence on internal security, civil liberties, and the Centre-State dynamics in law enforcement.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Reaffirms Right to Die with Dignity, Approves Withdrawal of Life Support
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court of India, in a landmark decision, approved the withdrawal of life support, including artificial nutrition and hydration, for Harish Rana.
• Harish Rana had been in a persistent vegetative state (PVS) for over 12 years following a head injury.
• The Court invoked the constitutional idea of dignity, stating that keeping terminally ill patients in PVS alive via technology is incompatible with dignity.
• This judgment expands the right to live with dignity under Article 21 of the Constitution to encompass the right to die with dignity.
• The Court also endorsed the concept of a 'living will', a legal document allowing individuals to state preferences for medical treatment if incapacitated.
• Previous landmark cases cited include Smt. Gian Kaur vs The State Of Punjab (1996) and the Aruna Shanbaug petition (2011), with a 2018 Constitution Bench ruling affirming the right to die with dignity.

The Supreme Court's latest pronouncement on the right to die with dignity is a significant step in upholding individual autonomy and human dignity, deeply rooted in Article 21 of the Constitution. By allowing the withdrawal of life support for patients in a persistent vegetative state, the judiciary has provided a compassionate framework for end-of-life care, addressing the ethical dilemmas posed by advanced medical technology. This judgment reinforces the principle of constitutionalism, ensuring that fundamental rights evolve to meet contemporary challenges. The endorsement of 'living wills' empowers citizens to make informed choices about their medical treatment, promoting patient-centric healthcare and reducing the burden on families. It also highlights the judiciary's role in filling legislative gaps in sensitive areas, balancing the sanctity of life with the right to a dignified death, and fostering a more humane approach to terminal illness.

GS2.headline: Global Economic and Geopolitical Shifts: India's FDI, China's Plan, US Diplomacy
GS2.explainer:
• Reports indicate a possible delay in former US President Donald Trump's visit to China, impacting geopolitical dynamics.
• India has introduced changes to its Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) policy, signaling potential shifts in its economic strategy.
• China is reportedly preparing to unveil its new Five-Year Plan, outlining its economic and social development goals for the coming period.
• These developments collectively highlight significant movements in international relations and economic policy across major global powers.
• The confluence of these events suggests evolving trade relations, investment landscapes, and strategic alliances.

The simultaneous developments concerning a potential delay in high-level US-China diplomatic engagement, India's adjustments to its Foreign Direct Investment policy, and China's upcoming Five-Year Plan underscore the dynamic nature of global geopolitics and economic governance. India's FDI policy changes are crucial for attracting capital, fostering economic growth, and integrating into global supply chains, reflecting its commitment to economic reforms and ease of doing business. Concurrently, China's Five-Year Plan will dictate its trajectory in areas like technological innovation, environmental sustainability, and social welfare, influencing global trade and investment patterns. The US-China diplomatic shifts, meanwhile, have broader implications for international stability, trade wars, and the balance of power, affecting multilateral institutions and global economic recovery efforts. These interconnected events necessitate a comprehensive understanding of international political economy and strategic foresight for policymakers.

GS2.headline: NATO Allies Reject US Demand for Hormuz Armada, Highlighting Transatlantic Divisions
GS2.explainer:
• Major NATO allies, including Germany and France, reportedly rejected a demand from the then-US administration (under Donald Trump) to send a naval armada to the Strait of Hormuz.
• The US had sought increased European military presence in the Persian Gulf to counter perceived threats from Iran.
• European nations expressed concerns that such a deployment could escalate tensions in the region and preferred a diplomatic approach.
• The Strait of Hormuz is a critical chokepoint for global oil shipments, with approximately one-fifth of the world's oil passing through it.
• The rejection highlighted growing transatlantic divisions and differing foreign policy priorities between the US and its European allies regarding Iran.

The refusal by key NATO allies to support a US-led naval deployment in the Strait of Hormuz underscores significant divergences in transatlantic foreign policy and security doctrines. This incident reflects the complexities of multilateral alliances, where national interests and strategic assessments can lead to differing approaches to global flashpoints. For India, a major energy importer, stability in the Persian Gulf and the unimpeded flow of oil through the Strait of Hormuz are paramount for its energy security. The episode highlights the delicate balance between coercive diplomacy and de-escalation in international relations, and the potential for unilateral actions by major powers to strain alliances and complicate regional stability, impacting global trade and economic interconnectedness.

GS2.headline: Global Implications of US Policy on Strait of Hormuz and Iran Tensions
GS2.explainer:
• The US administration's (under Donald Trump) assertive stance towards Iran, particularly concerning its nuclear program and regional activities, significantly heightened tensions in the Persian Gulf.
• The Strait of Hormuz, a narrow waterway connecting the Persian Gulf to the Arabian Sea, became a focal point for these tensions due to its strategic importance for global oil trade.
• Disruptions or threats to shipping in the Strait of Hormuz have immediate and far-reaching consequences for international energy markets and global economic stability.
• The US withdrawal from the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action (JCPOA) and subsequent imposition of sanctions on Iran were key drivers of the escalating crisis.
• The situation prompted concerns among international allies and trading partners about the potential for military confrontation and its ripple effects on global supply chains.

The escalating tensions surrounding the Strait of Hormuz, driven by the US's foreign policy approach towards Iran, exemplify how regional conflicts can rapidly acquire global dimensions. This geopolitical flashpoint, critical for international trade and energy security, highlights the interconnectedness of the global economy and the profound impact of major power politics on developing nations like India. The challenges to the rules-based international order, coupled with the complexities of non-proliferation and regional power dynamics, necessitate a nuanced diplomatic engagement from all stakeholders. For India, ensuring the stability of maritime trade routes and maintaining cordial relations with all parties in the Persian Gulf region is vital for its economic growth and strategic interests, underscoring the need for multilateral solutions to de-escalate such crises.

GS2.headline: EAM Jaishankar Engages EU Counterparts on West Asia, Ukraine, and Trade Deal
GS2.explainer:
• External Affairs Minister S. Jaishankar held discussions with European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen and EU Foreign Ministers in Brussels.
• Key topics included the geopolitical situations in West Asia and Ukraine, emphasizing shared objectives of de-escalation, stability, and energy security.
• Discussions also focused on the swift implementation of the India-EU trade deal, signed in January, covering trade, investment, technology, mobility, and defence.
• The visit underscored India's active diplomatic engagement with the 27-member European bloc on critical global and bilateral issues.
• Related discussions touched upon the Strait of Hormuz, highlighting its significance for energy security and global supply chains.

India's proactive diplomatic outreach to the European Union reflects its commitment to multi-alignment and strengthening strategic partnerships amidst evolving global power dynamics. The engagement on West Asia and Ukraine underscores India's role in promoting regional stability and its vested interest in global energy security, given its significant reliance on crude oil imports. The emphasis on accelerating the trade deal highlights India's economic diplomacy, aiming to enhance bilateral trade and investment, crucial for economic development and technological advancement. Such high-level interactions are vital for shaping India's foreign policy trajectory and reinforcing its position as a responsible global actor, contributing to a more multipolar world order and fostering cooperative federalism in international relations.

GS2.headline: Trinamool MPs Protest Election Commission's Removal of West Bengal Officials in Rajya Sabha
GS2.explainer:
• Trinamool Congress (TMC) MPs walked out of the Rajya Sabha on March 16, 2026, boycotting the day's sitting, to protest the Election Commission's (EC) removal of top bureaucrats in West Bengal.
• The EC had directed the removal of the Chief Secretary, Principal Secretary, and Home Secretary in West Bengal ahead of the Assembly polls in the state.
• TMC Rajya Sabha leader Derek O'Brien stated that while the EC has the power to do so, the party was protesting the manner and timing ("dead of night") of the removals.
• Parliamentary Affairs Minister Kiren Rijiju condemned the walkout, emphasizing that questioning a constitutional authority like the Election Commission in such a manner is not advisable.
• The incident highlights the powers of the Election Commission in ensuring free and fair elections and the dynamics between constitutional bodies and political parties.

This event underscores the critical role and constitutional autonomy of the Election Commission of India in upholding the integrity of the electoral process. The EC's power to transfer administrative officials during elections is a significant aspect of its mandate to ensure free and fair polls, often impacting Centre-State administrative relations. While parliamentary protests are a legitimate tool in a vibrant democracy, the debate surrounding the questioning of a constitutional authority's decisions raises important questions about parliamentary decorum and the separation of powers. This incident highlights the delicate balance between the executive, legislative, and independent constitutional bodies in India's federal structure, especially during election cycles, and its implications for good governance and electoral reforms.

GS2.headline: Madras High Court Dismisses Plea on Temple Land Arrears, Upholds HR&CE Act Provision
GS2.explainer:
• The Madras High Court dismissed a writ appeal filed by Amrutanjan Limited concerning rental arrears for land belonging to the Mylapore Kapaleeswarar Temple.
• The Hindu Religious and Charitable Endowments (HR&CE) department informed the court that Amrutanjan owed ₹9.74 crore in arrears for occupying 14 grounds of temple land until 2018.
• The land was originally leased for a 99-year period in 1901 at a monthly rent of ₹1,400, with Amrutanjan acquiring the lease rights from an assignee.
• Amrutanjan continued to occupy the property even after the 99-year lease expired on August 27, 2000, and subsequently challenged Section 34A(5) of the Tamil Nadu HR&CE Act, 1959.
• Section 34A(5) mandates the deposit of the claimed rental amount as a precondition for filing a statutory appeal before the HR&CE Commissioner.
• The High Court found no evidence that Amrutanjan had actually filed the statutory appeal or that the Commissioner had insisted on the deposit, leading to the dismissal of the writ appeal.

This Madras High Court judgment reinforces the statutory powers of state HR&CE departments in managing religious endowments and recovering outstanding dues, highlighting the critical importance of procedural adherence in legal challenges. It underscores the state's constitutional responsibility to protect public and institutional assets, particularly those belonging to religious trusts, against prolonged unauthorized occupation. The case also delves into principles of property law, the enforceability of lease agreements, and the legal framework governing religious institutions, ensuring accountability and financial integrity in their administration. The dismissal on grounds of non-compliance with statutory appeal requirements emphasizes the judiciary's role in upholding legal processes and preventing frivolous litigation.

GS2.headline: Regional Group Protests Railway Exam Language Policy, Demands Kannada Inclusion
GS2.explainer:
• The Karnataka Rakshana Vedike (KRV) announced protests to disrupt Railway promotion exams in Bengaluru and Hubballi on Tuesday.
• The protest is against the South Western Railway conducting exams only in Hindi and English, excluding Kannada.
• The exams are for 295 posts, including Goods Train Manager (60% promotional quota) and Limited Departmental Competitive Examination (LDCE) (15% promotional quota).
• Previously, following protests in 2008, the Railway Examination Board had begun holding exams in local languages, including Kannada, a practice now reportedly reversed.
• KRV president T. A. Narayana Gowda stated the protest would proceed despite a police notice, demanding resignation of Union Ministers.

This incident highlights the persistent tension between national language policies and regional linguistic aspirations, a critical aspect of India's federal structure and linguistic diversity. The demand for conducting central government recruitment examinations in regional languages underscores the principle of inclusive governance and equal opportunity, particularly for candidates from non-Hindi speaking states. The reversal of a previous policy allowing local languages in Railway exams raises questions about the Centre-State relations and the implementation of the Official Languages Act. Such issues often spark debates on cultural identity, administrative efficiency, and the constitutional guarantees related to language rights, impacting social cohesion and regional development. Ensuring equitable access to central government jobs across all linguistic groups is vital for fostering national integration and preventing regional alienation.

GS2.headline: Centre's Policy on Use of Beacon Lights on Official Vehicles Re-examined
GS2.explainer:
• In April 2017, the Union Cabinet banned the use of red beacon lights (lal batti) on all official vehicles, including those of the Prime Minister, President, and Chief Ministers.
• The ban came into effect from May 1, 2017, aiming to end VIP culture and promote equality.
• Only emergency services like ambulances, fire tenders, and police vehicles are permitted to use multi-coloured flashing lights.
• The Motor Vehicles Act, 1988, and subsequent rules empower the Central Government to specify who can use such lights.
• The decision was taken under the powers conferred by Section 109 of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988.

The prohibition on red beacon lights by the Union government in 2017 marked a significant step towards dismantling the vestiges of colonial-era VIP culture and fostering a sense of equality among citizens. This policy decision, rooted in principles of good governance and administrative reform, aimed to enhance public trust in institutions by demonstrating that no individual is above the law. While the move was widely lauded for promoting democratic values, its effective implementation across all levels of government, including state and local bodies, remains a continuous challenge. The debate often resurfaces when local officials are perceived to flout these national guidelines, highlighting the complexities of federal governance and the need for consistent enforcement to uphold the spirit of the law and ensure accountability in public life.

GS2.headline: Understanding 'No Quarter': Implications in International Humanitarian Law
GS2.explainer:
• "No Quarter" is a historical military term signifying a refusal to spare the lives of combatants who surrender, are captured, or are otherwise rendered hors de combat.
• Under modern international humanitarian law (IHL), specifically the Geneva Conventions and their Additional Protocols, giving "no quarter" is strictly prohibited.
• Combatants who surrender or are captured are considered prisoners of war (POWs) and are entitled to protection from violence, ill-treatment, and murder.
• The principle of distinction, proportionality, and humanity are fundamental tenets of IHL, aiming to limit the effects of armed conflict.
• Commanders who order or allow "no quarter" can be prosecuted for war crimes under international criminal law.

The concept of "no quarter," while historically present in warfare, stands in stark contrast to the foundational principles of modern international humanitarian law. Its prohibition is a critical aspect of the Geneva Conventions, which seek to humanize armed conflict by protecting non-combatants and those who are no longer participating in hostilities. This legal framework reflects a global consensus on the imperative to uphold human dignity even amidst the brutality of war, emphasizing the principles of distinction, proportionality, and humanity. Violations of this prohibition constitute war crimes, underscoring the accountability mechanisms within international criminal justice. Understanding such concepts is vital for appreciating the evolution of international law and its ongoing efforts to mitigate the suffering caused by armed conflicts and ensure respect for human rights.

GS3.headline: National Quantum Mission Advances: 23 Institutions Approved for Quantum Labs
GS3.explainer:
• Twenty-three academic institutions across India have received approval to establish quantum teaching laboratories.
• This initiative falls under the ambit of the National Quantum Mission (NQM), a flagship government program.
• The NQM was approved with a substantial budget of ₹6003.65 crore for the period 2023–2031.
• The mission's ambitious goals include developing quantum computers with capabilities ranging from 50 to 1,000 qubits.
• Other key objectives of the NQM are to achieve satellite-based secure quantum communication and develop high-precision quantum sensors and materials.
• An additional 100 proposals for quantum labs are currently under evaluation, indicating a broader push in this strategic technology.

The approval of quantum teaching laboratories under the National Quantum Mission signifies India's strategic commitment to fostering indigenous capabilities in cutting-edge technologies. This investment is crucial for national security, economic development, and maintaining technological sovereignty in the rapidly evolving global landscape. By focusing on quantum computing, secure communication, and advanced materials, India aims to position itself as a leader in the quantum domain, aligning with its vision of 'Atmanirbhar Bharat'. This initiative will not only drive research and innovation but also contribute to human resource development, creating a skilled workforce essential for leveraging the transformative potential of quantum science across various sectors, from healthcare to defense, thereby enhancing India's scientific temper and global competitiveness.

GS3.headline: Geopolitical Risks Drive Oil Price Volatility, Impacting Global Energy Security
GS3.explainer:
• Crude oil prices surged to $118 a barrel, reflecting heightened geopolitical risks, particularly from the West Asia conflict.
• This surge represents a more than 100% increase since mid-December 2025, when Brent crude was at $57.56.
• Unlike historical patterns where markets stabilized, current disruptions create tangible barriers to supply, fundamentally altering trade conditions.
• Key maritime chokepoints, including the Strait of Hormuz (20% of global oil consumption) and the Bab el-Mandeb/Suez corridor (10% of seaborne crude), are highly vulnerable.
• Geopolitical tensions inflate prices and increase maritime insecurity, leading to higher freight rates, insurance premiums, and tighter vessel availability.
• Oil has transformed into a political instrument, as seen with the redirection of Russian crude, introducing operational and financial volatility for importing nations.

The current volatility in global crude oil prices underscores the profound impact of geopolitical risks on energy security, moving beyond traditional supply-demand dynamics. For major importing economies like India, this shift has significant implications for fiscal stability, inflation management, and overall economic development. The vulnerability of critical maritime arteries highlights the imperative for diversified energy sources, robust supply chain resilience, and proactive diplomatic engagement to mitigate external shocks. This situation necessitates adaptable energy and trade strategies, emphasizing strategic petroleum reserves, renewable energy transition, and strengthening international partnerships. The transformation of oil into a political instrument further complicates global energy governance, demanding a nuanced foreign policy approach to safeguard national interests and ensure sustainable development.
